topic: Tidal M2 period — geometric self-contradiction + post-hoc structural test
change_summary: detail_verdict_text grew ~7x (149 → 1038 chars) since cycle 2 — added a post-hoc/structural rebuttal paragraph citing Part 3.1b, the trivial-transcription test, and a paraphrased dome-author quote about timestamped predictions. claim text was also shortened ~71% (51 → 15 chars). code_analysis.monitoring tag flipped from 'none' (cycle 2 verification) to 'hardcoded'. Re-review focuses on the new boilerplate paragraph for accuracy, applicability to a tidal WIN with no monitor.py code, and cross-WIN consistency.
our_argument_summary: WIN-045 argues the M2 tidal period (12.42h) is trivial — it is just half a lunar day, derivable from any model that takes the 24.84h lunar circuit as input. The deeper, geometric argument is that the dome's local moon at 2,534 km altitude produces a sharp single-spike tidal force field (9% of peak at 5,000 km offset, 0.6% at the equator, no far-side bulge), contradicting the observed global two-bulge semidiurnal pattern. The cycle-3 detail_verdict_text now carries a second, structural rebuttal: under our own post-hoc standard (Part 3.1b 'trivial-transcription test'), M2 is a textbook value with no pre-registration that meets our independence test, and the dome's derivation is algebraically equivalent to standard tidal theory. The verdict 'Self-Contradicted' is upheld on either argument alone; both together make this one of the most ironclad WIN dispositions in the review.

relevant_files: data/wins.json (WIN-045 entry; code_analysis.monitoring = 'hardcoded' as of latest)
raw-text/02-wins.txt (V50.6 snapshot, 2026-03-12 — does NOT contain tidal WINs; tidal WINs were V51.0 additions)
No current monitor.py snapshot in raw-text/ for V51.x; cannot directly verify whether tidal monitoring code was added between cycle 2 (2026-04-07) and now (2026-04-25)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Cycle 2 (2026-04-07) verified directly that monitor.py had no tidal domain — no fetch, no comparison, no hardcoded observed value. Current code_analysis.monitoring is 'hardcoded' which presupposes static expected AND observed values in monitor.py. Either (a) the dome added tidal monitoring code in the past 2.5 weeks and our raw-text snapshot is stale, or (b) the tag was changed without a code change and is wrong. The accompanying verdict prose ('hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call') asserts a fetch call exists; this is internally consistent with the 'hardcoded' tag but contradicts the cycle 2 verification. Recommend the analyst pull a fresh monitor.py snapshot to break the tie before relying on either tag.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Period values (M2 = 12.42h, etc.) are static assertions in wins.json. No derivation from dome geometry exists or is offered. The dome adopts the known oceanographic period and labels it confirmed. This is unchanged from cycle 2.
data_sources_used: M2 = 12.42 hours (NOAA tide-prediction tables; standard oceanographic reference; Doodson 1921)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Three known dome rescues, all weak: (1) 'aetheric tides work via a different mechanism' — unfalsifiable and unpublished; (2) 'period match alone is the prediction' — circular under our post-hoc standard; (3) 'observed tides are dynamical, not equilibrium' — true but the equilibrium-tide forcing field still has a two-bulge symmetry that the dome's one-spike field cannot produce regardless of dynamic complications.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: The verdict text's clause 'the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call' presupposes monitor.py code that cycle 2 verified did not exist for tidal WINs. Either the tag is wrong, the prose is wrong, or the dome added monitoring infrastructure post-cycle-2. Without a current monitor.py snapshot we cannot say which. This is a verifiable, falsifiable claim that should be either supported by code citation or rephrased.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Tag inherited from current wins.json. Cycle 2 (2026-04-07) verified there was NO tidal domain in monitor.py at that time and tagged 'none'. The flip to 'hardcoded' is unverified — raw-text/ in the repo is a V50.6 snapshot from 2026-03-12 (predates tidal WINs entirely). Recommend the analyst confirm by fetching the current monitor.py from the dome's GitHub before the next cycle.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: M2 = 12.42h has been textbook physics since Doodson (1921). The dome model contributes no new numerical value and offers no derivation from dome geometry — it adopts the standard oceanographic period and reframes its cause as 'aetheric.' This is the canonical relabel-and-claim pattern.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Tidal constituent periods have been published since 1921. Tidal WINs (045, 046, 049, 050, 051) entered the dome registry in V51.0 (April 2026) — a century after the value was tabulated. There is no pre-registration of the prediction in any timestamping venue independent of the dome author, and the value satisfies all three failure conditions of the trivial-transcription test (textbook reference, no independent timestamp, derivation algebraically equivalent to standard).
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula on the dome site starts from disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height_function H(r), or any aetheric parameter and arrives at 12.42 hours. The period derives from lunar_day / 2, where lunar_day is an astronomical input common to every model with a moon. The dome contributes no derivation step.

holes_found.description: Internal inconsistency between code_analysis.monitoring and the verdict prose. Current tag is 'monitoring: hardcoded' (which means: monitor.py contains static expected AND observed values for this WIN). The verdict text says 'the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call,' which asserts a live fetch call exists. But cycle 2 verified directly (2026-04-07) that monitor.py had NO tidal domain at all — no fetch, no comparison, no hardcoded values. We have no current monitor.py snapshot in raw-text/ to break the tie. If the cycle-2 verification still holds, the current tag should be 'none' and the prose's 'live fetch call' claim is factually wrong for this WIN. A dome defender who actually checks the dome's monitor.py for tidal code (and finds none) gets handed a free 'your review's structural argument is wrong' rebuttal.
holes_found.recommendation: The analyst should pull a fresh monitor.py from john09289/predictions, search for tidal/M2 domains, and either (a) confirm 'hardcoded' with file/line citation in code_analysis_evidence and keep the prose, or (b) revert the tag to 'none' and edit the verdict text to drop the 'same commit as live fetch call' clause for tidal WINs (the post-hoc argument doesn't need it — the trivial-transcription test alone suffices when there's no monitoring code at all).

holes_found.description: Cross-WIN inconsistency: the same boilerplate paragraph ('hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call') was pasted into WIN-050 and WIN-051 whose code_analysis.monitoring tag is 'none'. Within a single WIN entry this is a self-contradiction: tag claims no monitoring infrastructure, prose claims a fetch call exists. WIN-045/046/049 have tag='hardcoded' so the prose at least matches the tag, but the cycle-2 verification still casts doubt on the tag. The boilerplate was applied without per-WIN check of applicability.
holes_found.recommendation: Audit all five tidal WINs (045, 046, 049, 050, 051). For any WIN where monitoring='none' is correct, the 'live fetch call' clause must be removed and replaced with something like: 'No monitoring infrastructure exists for this WIN at all, so even the dome author's weaker timestamp test cannot apply — there is nothing to timestamp before.' This actually STRENGTHENS the rebuttal because it points out that a non-monitored WIN can't be 'prospective' under any definition. Recommend issuing this as a single multi-WIN patch rather than five separate patches.

holes_found.description: The dome author quote presented in quotation marks — '"prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"' — is a paraphrase, not a verbatim quote. The dome's actual phrasings are 'predictions made before confirming data,' 'predicted before data,' and 'a number derived from the model, registered with a timestamp, and subsequently confirmed by independent data' (raw-text/01-home.txt line 41 and 02-wins.txt line 10). A nitpicking dome defender can claim 'I never said that exact phrase' and use the misquote to dismiss the surrounding argument.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) replace the paraphrased quote with a verbatim one, e.g., 'a number derived from the model, registered with a timestamp, and subsequently confirmed by independent data' (then explain why hardcoded textbook values fail this test), or (b) drop the quotation marks and present it as a paraphrase: 'Even by the dome author's own weaker definition — a value committed to git before the data is fetched — ...'. Option (b) is cheaper and just as effective.

holes_found.description: Cycle 2 citation_check flagged a 'minor discrepancy' at the 9% figure, claiming inverse-cube with linear distance 7,534 km should give ~1.5%. That cycle-2 finding was wrong: it used h+d (linear sum) instead of √(h²+d²) (Pythagorean). Cycle 3 verification (this run) confirms the 9% figure is correct: at h=2,534 km altitude with 5,000 km horizontal offset, the actual distance is 5,605 km and (2,534/5,605)³ = 9.24% of peak. The 0.6% equator figure is also correct: distance √(2,534² + 14,000²) ≈ 14,227 km, ratio (2,534/14,227)³ = 0.56%.
holes_found.recommendation: No action needed on the prose. Note in the tracker that this cycle-2 'discrepancy' was a reviewer error, so it doesn't get re-flagged in future spot-checks.

holes_found.description: The detail_evidence still uses '~14,000 km' as the 'equator' distance from the sub-lunar point. This implicitly assumes the sub-lunar point sits at the dome's geometric center (north pole on the disc) and the equator at radius ~14,000 km. The dome model's actual sub-lunar point varies as the moon traverses, so '14,000 km' is really 'a typical mid-disc location.' The order-of-magnitude argument is unchanged either way (the equator point is always >5× the moon altitude away), but a careful dome defender could call this a strawman geometry.
holes_found.recommendation: Optionally rephrase '~14,000 km offset, near the geometric equator' as 'at a mid-disc location ~14,000 km from the sub-lunar point.' Low priority — not worth a patch on its own; bundle if other tidal-WIN edits land.

stronger_arguments.description: Tide-gauge spectrum sanity check. The Doodson harmonic analysis identifies M2 as the dominant constituent globally (open-ocean amplitude ~0.5–1 m), with S2 at ~0.15 m, K1 at ~0.15 m, etc. Critically, M2 is observed at near-equal amplitude in BOTH hemispheres — including Antarctic stations like McMurdo (Ross Sea M2 ~0.5 m) and Halley — and the two-bulge spatial pattern is recoverable from the global tide-gauge network. If the dome's local moon were correct, M2 would be hugely amplified directly under the moon's circuit and effectively absent at high southern latitudes. The actual data shows the opposite. This is a cleaner empirical test than the equilibrium-shape argument because it doesn't require solving Laplace's tidal equations on the disc.
sources: NOAA Tide Gauge Network: harmonic constituent tables for M2/S2/K1 at ~1,000+ stations globally
British Antarctic Survey Halley M2 amplitude measurements
Pawlowicz, Beardsley, Lentz (2002). 'Classical tidal harmonic analysis using T_TIDE.' Computers & Geosciences 28.

stronger_arguments.description: The 'one-pulse-per-day' prediction is directly falsifiable from any single coastal tide gauge, which makes the WIN-045 rebuttal especially robust. We could note that the falsification test is trivially reproducible: a reader with internet access can pull 24h of NOAA tide data from any station and count the high tides — they'll see two, not one. The dome model has no published mechanism that bridges its single-pulse forcing to a two-pulse observation. Stating this explicitly invites the reader to verify rather than trust us.
sources: NOAA Tides & Currents (tidesandcurrents.noaa.gov) — any station, any 24h window

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome model correctly identifies that tidal periods are arithmetically related to astronomical input periods (lunar day, solar day, lunar nodal cycle). This is the foundational insight of Doodson harmonic analysis. Recognizing M2 = lunar_day/2 demonstrates basic competence in tidal taxonomy.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Recognizing taxonomy is not prediction. Any model that ingests a 24.84h lunar circuit produces M2 by division. The discriminating outputs of a tidal theory are amplitudes, phases, and spatial patterns — and on all three the dome model is silent or contradicted. M2 = 12.42h is the input; the spatial two-bulge pattern is the output that distinguishes a distant-moon model from a local-moon one. The dome gets the input right and the output wrong, which is the opposite of what 'confirmed prediction' means.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 3 assessment: The Self-Contradicted verdict remains rock-solid. Both the geometric (one-spike-vs-two-bulges) and the structural (trivial-transcription post-hoc) arguments are independently sufficient. The cycle-3 detail_verdict_text expansion that now carries the structural argument is a strict improvement — readers who don't engage with the geometry argument can still reject the WIN on post-hoc grounds.

The issues found are all about precision of execution, not about the verdict:

1. The 'live fetch call' boilerplate clause was pasted into all five tidal WINs (045/046/049/050/051) and into WIN-029 (Schumann). It works for some WINs (where monitor.py actually has hardcoded values + a fetch call) and fails for others (where there's no monitor.py code at all). The decider's patch process needs a per-WIN applicability check, or — better — the boilerplate should be templated with a {% if monitoring == 'hardcoded' %} branch versus a {% if monitoring == 'none' %} branch, since the rhetorical move differs.

2. The monitoring tag drift on tidal WINs (cycle 2: 'none'; current: 'hardcoded') needs verification against current dome code. If the dome added tidal code in the past 2.5 weeks, that's a substantive change that warrants its own WIN-level update; if the tag was changed without a code change, it's a correctness regression.

3. The dome author quote should be either verbatim or unquoted. Current state — quotation marks around a paraphrase — is the worst of both worlds.

None of these issues threaten the verdict. They threaten the credibility of the rebuttal in the eyes of a careful adversarial reader. Fixing them is a 30-minute patch across five WINs; not fixing them gives a dome defender a thread to pull.

Quantitative verification (this cycle): all four numerical claims in the detail_evidence (9% at 5km offset, 0.6% at 14km, 6.6% globe variation, 10% globe tidal differential) check out to within rounding. The cycle-2 reviewer's 'minor discrepancy' flag on the 9% figure was a reviewer arithmetic error (linear distance instead of Pythagorean), not a flaw in the prose.

Confidence 0.92 (down from cycle-2's 0.97), reflecting the newly-discovered cross-WIN boilerplate inconsistency and the unverified monitoring-tag flip — both real bugs even though they don't move the verdict.

citations_verified: Doodson, A.T. (1921). Proc. Royal Society A, 100, 305–329. DOI 10.1098/rspa.1921.0088. ✓ Standard citation; URL resolves.
M2 = 12.42 hours: ✓ NOAA standard value, consistent across oceanographic references.
Inverse-cube tidal force ratio at 5,000 km offset (h=2,534 km): √(2534²+5000²) = 5,605 km, (2534/5605)³ = 9.24%. ✓ Matches review's '9% of peak' to within rounding.
Inverse-cube ratio at 14,000 km offset: √(2534²+14000²) = 14,227 km, (2534/14227)³ = 0.56%. ✓ Matches review's '0.6%' exactly.
Globe gravitational variation across Earth's diameter: (378022/390778)² = 6.42%. ✓ Matches review's '6.6%' to within rounding.
Globe tidal differential variation: (378022/390778)³ = 9.48%. ✓ Matches review's '~10%' to within rounding.
Cross-reference to Part 3.1b ('posthoc-standard'): ✓ The anchor exists in sections.json and contains the trivial-transcription test text.
citations_failed: Dome author quote '"prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled"': ⚠ This is a paraphrase but is presented in quotation marks. The dome's actual phrasings are 'predictions made before confirming data' (01-home.txt) and 'a number derived from the model, registered with a timestamp, and subsequently confirmed by independent data' (02-wins.txt). Recommend either verbatim quote or drop quotation marks.
citations_unchecked: Current monitor.py state (post-V51.0): not in raw-text/. Cannot verify whether tidal monitoring code was added between 2026-04-07 and 2026-04-25. Affects the 'monitoring: hardcoded' tag and the 'live fetch call' prose claim.
NOAA Antarctic tide-gauge M2 amplitudes: cited as supporting argument but not verified live this cycle.

advocate_mode.best_defense: An adversarial dome defender's strongest move on WIN-045 is not to defend the period (which is admittedly trivial) but to attack the geometric argument as a strawman about the wrong mechanism. The defense: 'The dome model has never claimed that tides are produced by direct Newtonian gravitational attraction at 2,534 km. The dome's tidal mechanism is aetheric resonance — the moon perturbs the firmament-cavity aether globally, much as the Schumann resonance fills the Earth-firmament cavity globally despite a single discharge source. Your inverse-cube calculation assumes the wrong physics. The two-bulge pattern emerges from global aetheric standing waves whose nodes are determined by cavity geometry, not by line-of-sight distance to the moon.' This defense is unfalsifiable as stated — but it's also rhetorically effective because it shifts the debate from 'where are the two bulges' to 'what is the dome's actual mechanism,' a question the review hasn't pre-empted because the dome model has not published an aetheric tidal mechanism.
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: Add one sentence to detail_evidence noting that the dome model has not published an aetheric tidal mechanism. Suggested wording: 'The dome model cites the M2 period as confirmed but has published no aetheric tidal mechanism — no equation linking the firmament cavity, the moon's altitude, and a tidal forcing field. Until such a mechanism is specified, the only tidal physics the dome inherits is gravitational, and gravitational physics on the dome's stated geometry produces a single-pulse, not a two-bulge, forcing field.' This preempts the 'aetheric tides work differently' rescue by demanding the dome cash the rhetorical check — without forcing a strong claim that no such mechanism could exist.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: WIN-050 and WIN-051 carry the same boilerplate '"the hardcoded expected value sits in the same commit as the live fetch call"' clause but tag code_analysis.monitoring as 'none.' Within a single WIN entry this is internally self-contradictory (the tag asserts no monitoring infrastructure; the prose asserts a fetch call exists). WIN-045/046/049 tag 'hardcoded,' which is at least internally consistent with the prose, but the cycle-2 verification (2026-04-07) found no tidal domain in monitor.py for any of the five WINs. WIN-029 (Schumann persistence) carries the same boilerplate but its monitoring='hardcoded' tag is well-attested by prior reviews of the Schumann domain. Net: the boilerplate is correct for ~1 of 6 affected WINs and incorrect or unverified for the other ~5. The decider should issue a single audit-and-patch ticket covering all six rather than continuing to apply the boilerplate without a per-WIN check.

claims_checked: Tidal force at 5,000 km horizontal offset = ~9% of sub-lunar peak (inverse-cube)
Tidal force at ~14,000 km offset (equator) = ~0.6% of peak
Globe gravitational variation across Earth's diameter = ~6.6%
quantitative_verification.computations: Python verification (this cycle): for moon altitude h=2,534 km and horizontal offset d, true distance = √(h²+d²); inverse-cube ratio = (h/√(h²+d²))³. d=5000 km: dist=5,605 km, ratio=9.24%. d=14,000 km: dist=14,227 km, ratio=0.56%. Globe: near-side r=378,022 km, far-side r=390,778 km; (near/far)²=0.9358 (6.42% variation, gravitational); (near/far)³=0.9052 (9.48% variation, tidal differential). M2: 24.84/2 = 12.42 ✓.
